skill.name: slack-gif-creator
skill.description: Create and validate animated GIFs optimized for Slack emoji and message use with local Python image utilities.
skill.summary: Build Slack-ready animated GIFs with local frame composition, easing, validation, and optimization helpers.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the C2, weak cryptography, network, and reconnaissance alerts are false positives from animation wording, constants, Markdown code fences, and Apache License text. The real risks are low: the skill provides executable Python examples, documents a pip install command, and writes GIF files to local paths. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, data exfiltration, or unauthorized network behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Malware Indicators Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines are animation easing constants, module descriptions, and Apache License text. No evidence found of cryptography, command-and-control behavior, or reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code contains animation math, descriptive text, or standard license wording. Review found no network endpoint, encryption routine, host probing, or control channel at those locations.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Blocks Misclassified as Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Most external command alerts are fenced Python examples and inline Python expressions in SKILL.md, not Ruby or shell backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding Markdown shows normal documentation examples for PIL drawing, GIF validation, easing, and animation concepts. They are not hidden command substitution or shell execution patterns.

low_findings.title: Dependency Installation Command Requires User Consent
low_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE low risk. The skill documents a pip install command for Pillow, imageio, imageio-ffmpeg, and NumPy. Installing packages can execute package manager behavior, but no hidden install script was found in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and limited to known image-processing dependencies. The risk is supply chain exposure from dependency installation, not malicious code in the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Local Filesystem Access for GIF Inputs and Outputs
low_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE low risk. The workflow opens local image files and writes GIF output paths. This is expected for GIF creation, but users should run it in trusted working directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file access is visible and directly related to image input and GIF output. There is no evidence that the paths are used for stealthy file discovery or exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Installation Instruction
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes an explicit pip install command. This should be treated as a user-approved setup step, not automatic execution by the skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is present in a dependency section and is not obfuscated. It still invokes an external package manager if the user runs it.

dangerous_patterns.title: Caller-Controlled GIF Output Path
dangerous_patterns.description: GIFBuilder.save accepts an output path and writes the generated GIF there. This is normal functionality but can overwrite files if used carelessly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The save path is caller-provided and used for local file output. The behavior is transparent and expected for a GIF builder.

content.value_statement: Slack GIFs often fail because dimensions, duration, or file size are wrong. This skill provides local utilities and guidance to build optimized animated GIFs.
actual_capabilities: Creates animated GIFs from PIL images or NumPy frame arrays.
Optimizes GIF dimensions, frame count, colors, and duplicate frames for Slack use.
Provides easing helpers for bounce, elastic, back, and linear motion.
Draws basic frame elements such as circles, text, stars, and gradient backgrounds.
Validates GIF dimensions, size, frame count, duration, and Slack readiness.
Documents recommended settings for Slack emoji GIFs and message GIFs.
limitations: It does not include prebuilt animation templates or stock graphics.
It depends on local Python packages such as Pillow, imageio, and NumPy.
It does not upload GIFs to Slack or manage Slack workspace settings.
Text rendering uses Pillow defaults unless the user adds font handling.

use_cases.title: Create team emoji reactions
use_cases.description: Build small looping GIFs that meet Slack emoji dimensions and keep file size under control.

use_cases.title: Produce lightweight message animations
use_cases.description: Generate short animated GIFs for announcements, demos, and visual status updates in Slack channels.

use_cases.title: Prototype image animations locally
use_cases.description: Use frame composition, easing, and validation utilities to test GIF concepts before sharing them.

prompt_templates.title: Make a simple Slack emoji GIF
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a small animated emoji.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 128x128 Slack emoji GIF of a pulsing star. Use simple shapes, 12 frames, and optimize it for Slack.

prompt_templates.title: Animate an uploaded image
prompt_templates.scenario: Use a local image as the visual source.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this uploaded image as the main object. Create a short Slack-ready GIF where it gently bounces and fades in.

prompt_templates.title: Validate and reduce a GIF
prompt_templates.scenario: Check an existing GIF and make it smaller.
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate this GIF for Slack emoji use. If it is too large, reduce frames, colors, or dimensions while keeping motion clear.

prompt_templates.title: Build a polished custom animation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request with motion and composition details.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Slack message GIF with a gradient background, moving text, bounce easing, and a particle burst. Keep it under three seconds.

output_examples.input: Create a Slack emoji GIF of a bouncing checkmark.
output_examples.output: A 128x128 optimized animated GIF with a checkmark that drops, bounces once, and settles cleanly for Slack emoji use.

output_examples.input: Make this uploaded logo spin for a Slack announcement.
output_examples.output: A short square GIF that rotates the logo smoothly, preserves the main colors, and reports file size and frame count.

output_examples.input: Check whether this GIF is ready for Slack.
output_examples.output: A validation summary covering dimensions, size, frame count, duration, and whether the GIF meets Slack emoji guidance.

best_practices: Use 128x128 dimensions for Slack emoji GIFs and keep loops short.
Reduce frame count and color count before lowering visual quality too far.
Validate the finished GIF before sharing it in a workspace.
anti_patterns: Do not assume emoji fonts will render consistently across platforms.
Do not create long or high-frame-rate GIFs for emoji reactions.
Do not install dependencies or write output files outside a trusted workspace.

faq.question: Does this skill upload GIFs to Slack?
faq.answer: No. It creates and validates local GIF files. Uploading to Slack is a separate user action.

faq.question: Can it use an uploaded image?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation shows how to open a local image with Pillow and use it in frames.

faq.question: What size should Slack emoji GIFs use?
faq.answer: The skill recommends 128x128 pixels for emoji GIFs and short loops under about three seconds.

faq.question: Does it include ready-made templates?
faq.answer: No. It provides utilities and animation concepts, while the user or assistant designs the specific animation.

faq.question: Which dependencies are needed?
faq.answer: The listed dependencies are Pillow, imageio, imageio-ffmpeg, and NumPy.

faq.question: Is network access required?
faq.answer: No runtime network behavior was found. The only reviewed URLs are standard Apache License references.
